name: melatonin
notes: reference template (CHK-2.1); 8 citations verified against PubMed. Strongest use is circadian timing (jet lag, DSPD); modest effect on sleep-onset; AASM advises against for chronic insomnia.

name: magnesium
sourceTarget: Mah & Pitre 2021 meta-analysis (BMC Complement Med Ther, PMID 33865376 — ~17 min SOL, GRADE low, n=151); Schuster 2025 bisglycinate RCT (DOI 10.2147/NSS.S524348, n=155, d=0.2); Hausenblas 2024 L-threonate RCT (Sleep Med X, DOI 10.1016/j.sleepx.2024.100121)
notes: the honesty test case. meta-analysis exists but small & low-quality; effect real but modest; form matters hugely (glycinate/threonate vs oxide bioavailability, §3.2). B, not A.

name: l-theanine
sourceTarget: Rao 2015; Hidese 2019 4-week RCT (200–400 mg, relaxation/quality) — note most trials are anxiety/relaxation-adjacent, sleep is a secondary outcome
notes: good safety, plausible mechanism (alpha-waves, no sedation). Effect is on subjective quality/latency, not a knockout. Dose-match usually fine.

name: ashwagandha
sourceTarget: Cheah 2021 systematic review/meta-analysis on sleep (PMID ~34559859 — verify); Langade 2019/2020 RCTs (root extract 600 mg); standardization = withanolide %
notes: §3.2 standardization central — KSM-66/Shoden standardized; generic 'ashwagandha 500 mg' often isn't. Safety: rare liver-injury case reports + thyroid/pregnancy cautions (sev-caution, note hepatotoxicity signal).

name: tart cherry
sourceTarget: Losso 2018 pilot RCT (insomnia, n=8 — tiny); Howatson 2012 (melatonin content + sleep); pooled reviews on tart cherry & sleep
notes: provisional B/C. Mechanism = actual melatonin + tryptophan/anti-inflammatory. Human trials SMALL. Dose-match: juice vs concentrate vs capsule varies wildly.

name: glycine
sourceTarget: Yamadera 2007 & Inagawa 2006 (3 g before bed, subjective quality + PSG — small Japanese RCTs, some industry-linked); Bannai/Kawai work
notes: provisional B/C. Decent mechanism (core body temp drop via peripheral vasodilation). Trials small/few, some Ajinomoto-funded — note conflict. C if replication thin.

name: valerian
sourceTarget: Bent 2006 systematic review (Am J Med, PMID 17145239 — mixed, methodological problems); Fernández-San-Martín 2010 meta-analysis; Cochrane-adjacent reviews
notes: classic 'popular > evidence.' Meta-analyses exist but heterogeneous, high risk of bias. §3.2 standardization (valerenic acid) all over the place. Honest C — do NOT inflate to B for fame.

name: lemon balm
sourceTarget: Cases 2011 (anxiety+sleep, small); often studied in combination (with valerian) → confounded
notes: provisional C/D. Mostly combination trials → hard to isolate. Thin.

name: gaba
sourceTarget: small RCTs (Byun 2018, latency); the crux is oral bioavailability / blood-brain-barrier penetration
notes: provisional C/D. Big mechanistic controversy — does oral GABA even cross the BBB? Lead with that honesty; the 'does it reach the brain' debate is the story.

name: 5-htp
sourceTarget: sleep RCTs sparse, often combined with GABA; key safety item = serotonergic interaction (SSRIs/MAOIs) + historical EMS concern
notes: SAFETY-heavy page — serotonin-syndrome risk, EMS history (sev-caution/serious). Efficacy thin → C, safety flag prominent.

name: magnolia bark
sourceTarget: mostly preclinical (honokiol GABA-A modulation); human sleep RCTs scarce; menopause-combination trials exist (confounded)
notes: provisional C/D — strong mechanism, weak human data → likely D unless human trials found.

name: l-tryptophan
sourceTarget: older meta-analyses (1980s, 1 g+ for latency); EMS/contamination history (1989) — safety
notes: provisional B/C. Has older RCT support for sleep latency (→ B upside), but data is dated + EMS safety history needs a clear note.

name: chamomile
sourceTarget: Zick 2011 (chamomile extract RCT, modest); Chang 2016 (postnatal women); apigenin mechanism preclinical
notes: provisional C/D. Separate chamomile-as-tea (cultural) from apigenin-as-isolated-compound. Human RCTs small/modest.

name: passionflower
sourceTarget: Ngan 2011 (tea, small); mostly small/combination trials
notes: popular in 'sleep blends,' minimal standalone human data → D. An honest example of a D done well.

name: hops
sourceTarget: almost always studied WITH valerian → confounded; standalone data scarce
notes: the valerian-hops combination confound is the whole story. D standalone.

name: lavender
sourceTarget: Silexan (oral lavender oil) anxiety RCTs (Kasper) — decent but anxiety-primary; aromatherapy sleep trials weaker/unblindable
notes: provisional C/D. IMPORTANT: separate oral Silexan (real RCTs, anxiety→sleep) from aromatherapy (weak, unblindable). Could split into two entries.

name: skullcap
sourceTarget: minimal human sleep data; note american vs chinese skullcap differ; hepatotoxicity reports (often adulteration)
notes: D. Safety note re: liver (historically tied to germander adulteration).

name: sleep blends
notes: LIVE at /sleep-blends — context/explainer, NOT a graded remedy. Shows the proprietary-blend penalty (§3.2): hidden doses make claims unfalsifiable, so a blend can't be graded on efficacy (F = transparency verdict). No efficacy citations; links to methodology + ingredient pages.

name: cbd
notes: legal/safety context is AU-specific; separate CBD-for-sleep evidence from CBD:THC ratios.

name: kava
sourceTarget: hepatotoxicity literature (Teschke reviews); anxiety RCTs (Pittler/Ernst — it DOES work for anxiety)
notes: model F entry. F is SAFETY-driven, not efficacy-driven — real anxiolytic evidence but documented hepatotoxicity, used for sleep off anxiety data. sev-serious callout. Proves F isn't 'doesn't work.'

name: cbt-i
sourceTarget: AASM clinical practice guidelines; Cochrane reviews; multiple meta-analyses (first-line for chronic insomnia)
notes: provisional S/A — first-line, strongest evidence in the whole catalog; likely the single highest-graded thing on the site. The thesis in action (behavior out-grades supplements).

name: sleep restriction therapy
sourceTarget: component of CBT-I with its own RCT base

name: stimulus control
sourceTarget: behavioral, good evidence base
notes: provisional A/B.

name: morning bright light
sourceTarget: circadian literature — strong for phase/timing
notes: provisional A/B.

name: warm bath before bed
sourceTarget: Haghayegh 2019 systematic review/meta-analysis (the 'warm-bath effect' has real RCTs)

name: breathing protocols
sourceTarget: HRV / relaxation data
notes: provisional C/B.

name: progressive muscle relaxation
sourceTarget: established relaxation-therapy evidence base

name: white noise
sourceTarget: mixed trials, some positive
notes: honest about weak/heterogeneous data.

name: weighted blanket
sourceTarget: small RCTs, anxiety-adjacent

name: sleep hygiene
notes: honest: weak AS A STANDALONE despite being everywhere.

name: blue-light glasses
sourceTarget: evening blue-light reduction trials — weaker than marketed
notes: provisional C/D — honest downgrade; model 'popular but overstated,' likely D.

name: phenibut
notes: F — dependence/withdrawal; safety must be prominent (sev-serious).

name: st john's wort
notes: interaction caution — CYP450 induction + serotonergic; safety-driven, not a sleep remedy per se.

name: iron (for rls)
notes: RLS-specific — iron/ferritin repletion; frame as context, not a general sleep aid.

name: homeopathic sleep aids
notes: the 'probably not' file — graded F/D with the evidence shown.

name: ramelteon
notes: Rx melatonin-receptor agonist — educational context page, not graded as a remedy.

name: when to see a doctor
notes: educational; points to clinicians (apnea/CPAP, RLS, chronic insomnia). Not graded.

name: melatonin in children
sourceTarget: pediatric ingestion/ER data (Lelak 2022 MMWR, already cited on melatonin page); TGA mislabeled-melatonin finding (verify)
notes: fold-in: a prominent safety section on the melatonin page PLUS a standalone context page — NOT a separately-graded remedy. Care around children, dosing-purity problems.
